name: Classical Mechanics
description: Newtonian, Lagrangian, and Hamiltonian mechanics. Conservation laws, forces, motion, energy, rigid-body dynamics, fluid mechanics, and chaos theory. The physics of the visible, human-scale world — and the foundation upon which all other physics is built. Predicts the trajectories of planets, the stability of bridges, and the flow of rivers.
axioms: F = ma (Newton's second law — net force equals mass times acceleration)
Newton's first law: objects maintain uniform motion unless acted upon by a net force (inertia)
Newton's third law: every action has an equal and opposite reaction
Conservation of energy: total energy in an isolated system is constant
Conservation of momentum: total linear momentum in an isolated system is constant
Conservation of angular momentum: total angular momentum is constant under central forces
Principle of least action: physical paths extremize the action functional S = ∫L dt
Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = -GMm/r²
reasoning_patterns: force decomposition: resolve forces into components, apply F=ma along each axis
energy conservation: when forces are conservative, use E = T + V rather than integrating F=ma
phase space analysis: plot trajectories in (q, p) space to identify fixed points and cycles
symmetry → conserved quantity: time symmetry → energy; translation → momentum; rotation → angular momentum (Noether)
Lagrangian method: choose generalized coordinates, write L = T - V, solve Euler-Lagrange equations
Hamiltonian method: Legendre transform to (q, p), solve Hamilton's equations of motion
Small oscillations: linearize about equilibrium, find normal modes and frequencies
Chaos detection: compute Lyapunov exponents for non-integrable systems
boundary_conditions: Breaks down at quantum scale (< atomic diameters)
F = ma is invalid for variable mass systems (requires careful momentum accounting)
Determinism breaks for chaotic systems despite deterministic equations
Does not cover quantum superposition, entanglement, or wave-particle duality
key_references: Landau & Lifshitz. Mechanics (Course of Theoretical Physics Vol.1)
Feynman, R. The Feynman Lectures on Physics Vol.1
Arnold, V.I. Mathematical Methods of Classical Mechanics
Newton, I. Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica (1687)
canonical_truths: Newton's laws reproduce Kepler's planetary laws exactly (gravitational inverse-square law)
Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulations are equivalent to Newtonian but more general
Simple harmonic oscillator is the most important model — it approximates nearly all stable equilibria
Chaotic systems are deterministic but unpredictable beyond Lyapunov timescale
The three-body problem has no general closed-form solution (proved by Bruns-Poincaré)
